props.covers: The editing face at packages/canvas/src/stage/editor/: the composition root (InteractiveCanvasEditor), the interaction pipeline (a pure nine-state dispatcher with effects quarantined at the hook edge, DOM hit resolution, two rAF loops, and slot-injected feedback visuals), the nine feature slices, the viewport hook, and the hotkey map.

text.insert: The editor is a layer on top of the stage core:

with the viewport and hotkey hooks, runs every gesture through the interaction pipeline, and mounts the chrome. Everything ephemeral the editor draws — selection boxes, drag rectangles, snap guides, ghosts — arrives in the stage through its overlay slots; the stage itself only ever draws the document (see
, which also owns the read-only viewer face). Inside the editor every feature is a vertical slice: its gesture logic and its visuals live in one folder named after the feature.
is the editing composition root. It accepts the document plus save/cancel/change and shell props, then owns the live editing session with
nested in the transformed world slot.
is the editor-owned React hook wrapping the stage viewport helpers documented in
with real pointer and wheel handling. It owns
, fits to document content once on mount when the stage is measurable, and exposes
helper, all kept current through refs rather than listener churn.
Wheel handling distinguishes plain scroll (pan) from ctrl/meta-modified scroll (zoom toward the cursor — trackpad pinch also arrives as a
wheel event in browsers). Rapid wheel events are coalesced via

into at most one viewport commit per animation frame: the hook accumulates pan deltas and multiplicative zoom factors into a pending frame, then applies the accumulated frame using the latest cursor point. This wheel coalescer is separate from the pipeline's drag coalescer — both are
instances, but the viewport hook accumulates wheel deltas while the pipeline coalesces latest
Panning is also available via space-held-drag or middle-mouse-drag (tracked with raw
listeners for space state, focus/visibility reset guards, and pointer capture for the drag itself), or via plain left-drag when the hook is constructed with
(the editor's hand tool). This hook — not
— is the sole owner of space-drag panning.
listener for the editor's whole hotkey vocabulary, kept in sync with UI hint text by the file header. The dock keys are the left-hand homerow (
, plus Cmd/Ctrl-A for the sticky tool), so tool switching never leaves the keyboard's left hand;
object tools on top. The full binding table, reproduced from that header:
— Shapes panel (same path as the Shapes dock button)
— connector dock tool (arms dedicated Connector Mode)
— copy selection to the in-memory clipboard
by 1 world unit (snap: false); Shift+Arrow moves by
— cancel the active interaction machine gesture, else close an open context menu, else exit placement/connector mode back to select, else clear selection
is an input, textarea, select, or contentEditable element (

callback reports true — the editor wires this to whether the in-place text editor or connector-label editor currently has focus. Both checks exist because the inline editors own the keyboard while focused, but focus can lag a render, so the callback is a defensive second guard. Space is deliberately NOT bound in this hook at all —
owns space-drag panning separately, with its own space-held tracking. Anyone adding a new binding to

or the keydown handler gets this scoping for free by construction (the guard runs first, before any key is dispatched) — but a new hotkey that needs to fire even while typing (there currently are none) would need its own explicit carve-out, not a change to the shared guard.
. Feature-slice suites from the requested glob are

. Dedicated suites cover selection, resize, move, place, snapping, section-fit, selection-toolbar, text-editing, and context-menu; the machine's spec-level tests live mostly in those slice suites plus the pipeline hit/hover tests.
For dock, toolbar, palette, and other chrome components wired into
, and the lower-level pointer/hit-test vocabulary lives in
owns both the object text editor and the connector-label editor. Existing-object entry is double-click driven and gated by the effective def's
; connector labels open from connector double-click and anchor at
. The editor overlay reuses the def's
descriptor so mid-edit and at-rest text are pixel-identical under the D14 principle, while
suppresses the at-rest render underneath and the active editors opt back into
is the sticky live-markdown path with a native
listener that maps edits back to markdown source. The pipeline's
one-shot lets gestures such as double-click and quick connect request an editor session, including predicted IDs for objects that do not exist in React state yet.
Color controls stay out of this bundle: palette UI lives in
, while object-def color semantics belong with
is a pure switch over the nine-kind
. It delegates one-for-one to the per-slice
is the sub-threshold gate: a press becomes a click on release, a move when an object drag reaches
world px), or a drag-select from empty canvas.
is pure as well; it returns restore actions for active move/resize and otherwise idles with no dispatch.

props.title: The dispatcher is pure; effects live at the hook edge

text.insert: Decision (accepted): The dispatcher is pure; effects live at the hook edge —
take (state, event, read-only ctx) and return a result object; no I/O, no React, no refs. The only place effects happen is
: assign the state ref, publish the overlay, loop the dispatch array into the reducer, and fire the
one-shot. That keeps every gesture unit-testable as data in → data out, and it is why the gesture suites run without DOM.
, seeds the latest pointer snapshot, starts edge-pan, and steps a normalized
listeners for its lifetime and guards them with the active pointer ref, so fast drags outside the stage still land.
normalizes native events into screen coordinates via
walks DOM attributes in priority order:
→ endpoint → port → screen-space anchor-dot proximity → bend-segment → connection-id → section-title-chip → object-id → the world-space
fallback → canvas. Source nuance: endpoint, bend-segment, and connection branches use
because those targets can be SVG nodes; ports are HTML elements in this DOM. A DOM-matched object is vetoed when the pointer is outside its true outline (
, the D16 rule), falling through to the world hit-test; section title chips bypass that veto.

records the latest snapshot, commits at most once per frame, uses latest-wins semantics, flushes before pointer-up/cancel so the final position is never dropped, and cancels on unmount or Escape. The second loop is a self-rescheduling edge-pan tick: it engages only for

, then re-pushes the last snapshot so the gesture re-steps against the moved viewport. Both rAF closures read refs updated every render, so once-created callbacks see current document, selection, tool, viewport, and callbacks.
InteractionFeedback: slice visuals through the stage slots
renders in the transformed world layer: the placement ghost is the drafted object rendered through the real
— each gated on its overlay field. The editor mirrors the pipeline overlay into React state so the slots re-render; this composition is the stage slot conversion.
tracks hover for the connector tool only.

with a section-outline tolerance and a sticky expanded-bounds halo, so a hovered object keeps its anchor dots while the cursor travels to them; the result feeds port-proximity hits and the anchor-dot set.

owns a feature end to end: gesture steppers, slice-local state types, and the visuals that render through InteractionFeedback. Slices export a small surface and never import each other sideways — the one declared exception is
on empty canvas with the select tool; Shift makes the marquee additive, and
enter immediately from a handle hit with no click semantics;
are the screen overlay selection visuals.
folds section descendants into the moved set,
flows through the overlay into the
drop-target prop, and live snapping imports
maps tools to object types; click placement uses default centered geometry, while drag placement clamps each dimension to
is only the dashed screen fallback when no draft object is present.
is the MPL-headered AFFiNE distribution port with
is a host-injection seam that production does not supply; only the snap-inject test uses it, and live drag snapping runs
resolves the toolbar from the current selection and anchors it with
for FigJam parity, and also hides in connector mode; toolbar content and flyout chrome belong to

, which retargets right-clicks through the true outline before falling back to the canvas menu. Any primary stage pointer-down closes an open menu on the select/connector paths; Escape closes it after gesture cancellation. The fit-section item is section-only and disabled/no-ops when
is the optional right-side panel rendered by
is true. It exposes object text, geometry nudges/sizing, color, annotations, connection details, delete, and the same section-fit affordance as toolbar and context menu, gated by
is an rAF tween from the section's current geometry to
) unless reduced motion or missing frame APIs force an immediate commit.
disables every fit affordance when the section already fits, and the pipeline triggers the animation from double-click on a section body. There is no section dimming visual here.
